summary: The current benchmark entry point is the Node harness in benchmarks/run-benchmark.mjs, while the strongest ordinary Clasp execution evidence is already through native claspc run scenarios. The smallest benchmark-facing gaps are not broad compiler redesigns; they are standardizing the ordinary-program workflow as a benchmark task, avoiding shell-only wrappers in examples used as evidence, and keeping process/runtime results structured and durable from Clasp code.

ordinary_clasp_execution_path.behavior: claspc run builds or reuses a cached native backend binary, passes args after -- to the program, and executes the binary directly. The program owns durable state through runtime file/process/swarm builtins.
scenario_evidence: examples/feedback-loop/CodexLoopDemo.clasp invokes codex exec directly from Clasp and persists codex-loop-status.json plus step artifacts.
examples/feedback-loop/MonitoredWorkflowDemo.clasp runs direct executable command arrays, captures stdout/stderr/exit status, logs events, and persists workflow-status.json.
examples/swarm-native/Swarm.clasp exposes the native swarm substrate to ordinary Clasp programs without requiring external claspc swarm subprocesses.
benchmarks/tasks/clasp-legal-assistant-appbench/repo/scripts/verify.sh verifies repeated ordinary claspc run commands against durable app state.

benchmark_facing_gaps: The publication harness is still Node/shell-first. That is acceptable for benchmark orchestration, but the first ordinary-agent-loop benchmark should make the Clasp program itself the workflow owner under claspc run rather than treating Clasp as code generated for a wrapper.

Some orchestration evidence still uses bash -c inside command arrays for fixture convenience. For the no-shell-wrapper claim, use direct executables or a fake codex binary as scripts/test-codex-loop-program.sh does.

The process and swarm APIs are available through foreign/builtin surfaces and example modules, but there is not yet a single small stdlib-like Process/Swarm API package that benchmark task repos can import without copying local wrappers.
The native durable substrate is ahead of the JavaScript emitter. Do not expand backend-native work into JavaScript parity unless the benchmark scenario truly compiles to a frontend/Node target.

Dict/map state remains a language ergonomics gap. Current state-heavy code works around this with records plus lists and linear scans; empty lists, polymorphic collections, record update, and destructuring have working examples.
recommended_acceptance_checks: Ordinary Codex loop: timeout 180 bash scripts/test-codex-loop-program.sh. It should prove no bash string in CodexLoopDemo.clasp, direct codex exec invocation, persisted final status, event log, prompts, stdout, stderr, heartbeat, and final-message artifacts.

Monitored workflow: timeout 180 bash scripts/test-monitored-workflow.sh. It should prove direct executable command arrays, structured stdout/stderr/exit capture, failure handling, event logging, heartbeat artifacts, and durable final status.

Native control substrate from Clasp: timeout 180 bash scripts/test-swarm-native-managed-loop.sh. It should prove ordinary Clasp can create objectives/tasks/DAGs, set merge policies, run builder/verifier commands, record artifacts, approve, decide mergegates, and persist report state.

Benchmark task entry: add or target a benchmark task whose verify command runs claspc run on an ordinary Clasp workflow program and asserts final state artifacts, not just compiled JS or an external shell workflow.

Ergonomics smoke: run claspc check/run for examples/polymorphism/Main.clasp and examples/record-ergonomics/Main.clasp when state-heavy orchestration code changes; treat Dict/map support as a follow-up only if the next benchmark actually needs keyed lookup.
residual_risks: I did not run bash scripts/verify-all.sh per task instruction.
The scout did not add a new benchmark task; W1-T3 is the natural implementation branch for the ordinary-program loop benchmark.
Dict/map support remains unclosed as a language feature, but current benchmark-facing orchestration examples do not require it to prove the ordinary-program loop path.
